options: B+ tree index
B-tree index
Hash index
Dense index
explanation: Hash indexes are ideal for equality queries (find exact key) with O(1) average time, but they scatter keys randomly via the hash function, making range queries (find all keys between a and b) require a full scan.

q: The number of disk I/Os to search in a B+ tree with n records and order m is approximately:
options: O(n)
O(log m n)
O(n/m)
O(m log n)
explanation: A B+ tree of order m has height approximately log_m(n) (logarithm base m of n records). Each level requires one disk I/O (one page read), so search cost is O(log_m n) disk I/Os â€” very efficient for large datasets.

q: A clustered file organization stores:
options: All records of one relation sorted by primary key
Records of related tables physically together on disk
Only index entries without actual data
Records in random hash-based order
explanation: A clustered file organization physically co-locates records from related tables (e.g., parent and child rows in a join) on the same disk blocks. This optimizes join performance by reducing disk seeks for related data.

q: In the context of indexing, a 'covering index' is one where:
options: The index covers all tables in the database
The query can be answered entirely from the index without accessing the data file
The index covers both primary and secondary keys
The index is replicated across multiple disks
explanation: A covering index contains all the columns needed by a query in its index structure. The query engine can satisfy the query entirely from the index without additional lookups to the actual table data, reducing I/O.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about a dense primary index?
options: It has one index entry per data block
It has one index entry per record
It requires data to be unsorted
It uses hashing for fast lookup
explanation: A dense index has one entry for every record (every key value) in the data file. When it is also a primary index (on the ordering key), it provides direct access to every record. Dense indexes require more storage than sparse indexes.

q: The MRU (Most Recently Used) page replacement policy is preferred for:
options: Random access workloads
Sequential scan workloads
Hash join operations
Index range scans
explanation: For sequential scans (reading each page once and never again), MRU is better than LRU. Once a page is processed in a sequential scan, it won't be needed again soon, so evicting the most recently used page is optimal.

q: A secondary index on a non-unique field requires:
options: One index entry pointing to all records with that key value
One index entry per unique key value with pointer to a bucket of record pointers
Sorting the data file on the secondary key
At least two levels of indirection
explanation: For non-unique secondary indexes, each index entry for a key value points to a bucket (or linked list) of pointers to all records with that value. This avoids storing duplicate keys in the index while accessing all matching records.

q: The term 'fill factor' in B+ tree index creation refers to:
options: Percentage of a node's capacity used when initially populated
Ratio of leaf nodes to internal nodes
Maximum number of keys allowed per node
Compression ratio of index storage
explanation: Fill factor specifies what percentage of each index page/node is filled during initial creation (e.g., 80% fill factor). Leaving some space allows future insertions without immediate page splits, improving write performance.

q: In ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method), overflow records created by insertions are stored in:
options: The main sequential file by shifting records
A separate overflow area linked to the main file
A new B-tree structure created dynamically
The index nodes directly
explanation: ISAM has a static index structure. When a page is full and new records arrive (insertions), they go into overflow pages/chains linked to the original page. Over time, many overflows degrade performance â€” unlike dynamic B+ trees.

q: Which storage level in the memory hierarchy has the HIGHEST access speed?
options: Main memory (DRAM)
L3 Cache
L1 Cache (CPU registers/cache)
NVRAM (Non-volatile RAM)
explanation: L1 Cache (and CPU registers) are the fastest storage, typically within the CPU chip itself with sub-nanosecond access. L1 is faster than L2, which is faster than L3, which is faster than main memory (DRAM, ~100ns access).

q: Buffer manager's 'write-ahead logging' (WAL) rule requires:
options: Writing data pages before log records
Writing log records to disk before the corresponding dirty data page
Writing all dirty pages before committing a transaction
Writing index pages before data pages
explanation: WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) requires that log records for a change must be written to stable storage BEFORE the corresponding dirty data page is written to disk. This ensures crash recovery is possible using the log.

q: A B-tree index node with order 5 can have at most how many child pointers?
explanation: In a B-tree of order m (also called degree or order 5), each node can have at most m=5 children (child pointers). A node with k keys has k+1 child pointers, and max keys = m-1 = 4, so max children = 5.

q: The transfer rate of a disk depends on:
options: Seek time and rotational latency only
RPM and track density (data density)
Buffer pool size and disk cache
Number of disk platters only
explanation: Data transfer rate depends on how fast the disk spins (RPM â€” higher RPM means more data passes under the head per second) and how densely data is stored on the track (areal/linear density). Transfer time = Block size / Transfer rate.

q: A multilevel index is created to:
options: Allow indexes on multiple columns simultaneously
Reduce the size of the index by indexing the index itself
Create both clustered and non-clustered indexes
Store index and data in the same file
explanation: A multilevel index treats the first-level index file as a sorted file and builds another (outer) index on it. This reduces the number of disk accesses needed. B+ trees are essentially an elegant multilevel index implementation.

q: In disk storage, a 'cylinder' refers to:
options: A single track on one platter
The set of all tracks at the same radial position across all platters
The physical disk enclosure
A unit of data block storage
explanation: A cylinder consists of all tracks at the same radial distance from the spindle across all disk platters. Reading data within the same cylinder requires no seek (head movement), so data in the same cylinder is accessed with zero seek time.

q: Which page replacement policy is theoretically optimal but impractical in real systems?
options: LRU (Least Recently Used)
MRU (Most Recently Used)
CLOCK (Second Chance)
Optimal (Replace page used farthest in future)
explanation: The Optimal (OPT/BÃ©lÃ¡dy's) algorithm evicts the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. It gives the minimum possible page faults but requires future knowledge of page references, making it impractical.

q: In a hash index with linear probing for collision resolution, the worst-case search time is:
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(nÂ²)
explanation: With linear probing, if many keys hash to the same or adjacent slots (clustering), a search may need to probe through many slots sequentially. In the worst case (heavily loaded table), this degrades to O(n) â€” examining all n slots.

q: A sparse index on a file with 1000 blocks (one record per block, file is sorted) would have approximately how many index entries?
options: One per block = 1000
explanation: A sparse index typically has one entry per disk block (not per record). If the file has 1000 blocks (one record per block here), the sparse index has 1000 entries â€” one pointing to the first record of each block.

q: The primary advantage of a B+ tree over a binary search tree for database indexes is:
options: B+ tree allows duplicate keys, BST does not
B+ tree has higher degree (branching factor), resulting in shallower tree and fewer disk I/Os
B+ tree operations are O(1) while BST is O(log n)
B+ tree does not require balancing unlike BST
explanation: B+ trees have high branching factor (hundreds of children per node) matching disk page size, resulting in very shallow trees (height 3-4 for millions of records). This minimizes disk I/Os per lookup, unlike BSTs which need O(logâ‚‚n) disk accesses.

q: In buffer management, the 'clock' (second chance) replacement algorithm approximates:
options: FIFO replacement
LRU replacement
MRU replacement
Random replacement
explanation: The CLOCK algorithm approximates LRU using a reference bit per frame. When a page is accessed, its bit is set to 1. When replacement is needed, a clock hand sweeps: if bit=1, set to 0 and continue; if bit=0, evict. This mimics LRU cheaply.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about heap file organization?
options: Insertion is expensive but search is fast
Insertion is fast but search requires full scan
Both insertion and search are O(log n)
Records are maintained in sorted order
explanation: Heap file (unordered file) appends new records at the end â€” insertion is O(1) (very fast). However, searching for a specific record requires scanning all records in the worst case â€” O(n). No ordering is maintained.

q: A database index significantly speeds up queries but has a cost of:
options: Reduced query parallelism
Extra disk space and slower insert/update/delete operations
Preventing the use of joins
Requiring exclusive locks during reads
explanation: Indexes occupy additional disk space (for the index structure) and must be updated on every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operation affecting the indexed column. This write overhead is the tradeoff for faster SELECT query performance.

q: In indexed sequential file organization, overflow handling is a challenge because:
options: The hash function may produce collisions
Insertions into a full block require chained overflow pages degrading sequential access
The B+ tree must be rebalanced after every insertion
Index entries cannot accommodate new key values
explanation: Indexed sequential files maintain sorted order and a static index. When a block is full, new records go to overflow chains, breaking the physical sequential order. Periodic reorganization (rebuilding) is needed to restore performance, unlike dynamic B+ trees.

q: In log-based recovery, which log record is written BEFORE a data item is modified on disk?
options: Checkpoint record
Commit record
Before-image (BFIM) in the log
Redo record
explanation: In log-based recovery, a before-image (BFIM) â€” the old value of a data item â€” is written to the log before the actual modification is applied to disk. This ensures that if a failure occurs, the system can use UNDO to restore the original value.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the UNDO operation in log-based recovery?
options: It reapplies all committed transactions
It restores the database to a state before an uncommitted transaction modified it
It writes new values to the database for committed transactions
It creates a new checkpoint in the log
explanation: UNDO restores the database to its state before an uncommitted (or incomplete) transaction made changes. It uses the before-image stored in the log to reverse modifications made by transactions that did not commit.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the REDO operation in log-based recovery?
options: It reverses changes made by uncommitted transactions
It reapplies changes of committed transactions that were not yet written to disk
It removes log records of aborted transactions
It creates a new shadow page
explanation: REDO reapplies the changes of committed transactions whose updates may not have been physically written to disk before a crash. It uses the after-image (AFIM) stored in the log to restore the committed state.

q: In immediate database modification, when are changes written to the database?
options: Only after the transaction commits
Only during checkpointing
While the transaction is still active (before commit)
Only after a REDO pass
explanation: In immediate database modification, changes to the database may be written to disk while the transaction is still active â€” i.e., before the commit. This requires UNDO capability to reverse changes if the transaction aborts.

q: In deferred database modification, changes are written to the database:
options: Before the transaction begins
While the transaction is executing
Only after the transaction successfully commits
During the UNDO pass of recovery
explanation: In deferred database modification (also called deferred update), all changes are kept in the log and only written to the actual database after the transaction commits. This eliminates the need for UNDO since uncommitted changes never reach the database.

q: Which logging protocol requires that a log record be written to stable storage BEFORE the corresponding data page is written to disk?
options: Redo logging
Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)
Shadow paging
Checkpoint logging
explanation: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) mandates that log records must be written to stable storage before the corresponding dirty data pages are flushed to disk. This ensures that the log always has enough information to recover the database after a failure.

q: A checkpoint in database recovery is used to:
options: Create a full backup of the database
Reduce the amount of work needed during recovery by marking a known consistent state
Delete all old log records permanently
Switch from immediate to deferred modification
explanation: A checkpoint flushes all dirty buffers to disk and writes a checkpoint record to the log. During recovery, the system only needs to process log records after the last checkpoint, significantly reducing recovery time.

q: During crash recovery using the ARIES algorithm, the three phases executed are:
options: Backup, Restore, Verify
Analysis, Redo, Undo
Checkpoint, Rollback, Commit
Scan, Write, Flush
explanation: ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics) uses three phases: Analysis (scan log to find dirty pages and active transactions), Redo (replay history to reconstruct the database state at crash time), and Undo (roll back all incomplete transactions).

q: In UNDO/REDO logging, which rule ensures that undo is possible after a crash?
options: Log record written after data is committed
Before-image logged before data page written to disk (WAL rule)
After-image logged after checkpoint
No log records needed for reads
explanation: The WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) rule ensures the before-image is logged before the data page is written to disk. This guarantees that UNDO can reverse uncommitted changes even if the system crashes after writing data but before committing.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about shadow paging recovery?
options: It uses a log to record before and after images
It maintains two page tables: a current page table and a shadow page table
It requires WAL protocol
It is primarily used in ARIES
explanation: Shadow paging maintains two page tables: the shadow page table (stable, unchanged copy) and the current page table (modified during the transaction). On commit, the shadow is replaced; on abort, the current table is discarded, restoring the shadow state without needing a log.

q: What is the main disadvantage of shadow paging compared to log-based recovery?
options: It cannot handle system crashes
It causes data fragmentation and is inefficient for large databases with many concurrent transactions
It does not support UNDO operations
It requires more log space
explanation: Shadow paging suffers from data fragmentation because pages are copied rather than updated in-place, and it does not support efficient concurrent access for multiple transactions. It is generally less efficient than log-based recovery for large, concurrent workloads.

q: RAID level 0 is characterized by:
options: Mirroring without striping
Striping without redundancy
Block-level striping with parity
Byte-level striping with mirroring
explanation: RAID 0 uses disk striping â€” data is split across multiple disks to improve performance. There is NO redundancy or fault tolerance; if any disk fails, all data is lost. It offers the best write/read performance but no reliability.

q: RAID level 1 provides fault tolerance through:
options: Parity bits distributed across disks
Disk striping with dedicated parity
Disk mirroring â€” each disk has an exact duplicate
XOR parity across all drives
explanation: RAID 1 (disk mirroring) maintains an exact duplicate of each disk. If one disk fails, the mirror provides an immediate replacement. It offers excellent fault tolerance and read performance but 50% storage overhead.

q: Which RAID level uses byte-level striping with dedicated parity?
options: RAID 0
RAID 1
RAID 2
RAID 3
explanation: RAID 3 uses byte-level (or bit-level) striping across multiple data disks with a single dedicated parity disk. All read/write operations access all data disks simultaneously, making it suitable for large sequential transfers but less suited for random I/O.

q: RAID level 5 distributes parity information across all disks. What is the minimum number of disks required for RAID 5?
explanation: RAID 5 requires a minimum of 3 disks. Parity is distributed across all disks (not dedicated to one), allowing the system to tolerate the failure of any single disk. It balances performance, storage efficiency, and fault tolerance.

q: RAID level 6 differs from RAID 5 in that it:
options: Uses mirroring instead of parity
Stores two independent parity blocks, tolerating failure of up to two disks
Uses dedicated parity disk
Provides no fault tolerance
explanation: RAID 6 uses double distributed parity (two independent parity calculations, e.g., P and Q), allowing the array to survive the simultaneous failure of any two disks. It requires a minimum of 4 disks and has higher write overhead than RAID 5.

q: Which RAID level combines mirroring (RAID 1) and striping (RAID 0)?
options: RAID 2
RAID 3
RAID 4
explanation: RAID 10 (also written RAID 1+0) combines disk striping (RAID 0) for performance with disk mirroring (RAID 1) for redundancy. It requires a minimum of 4 disks and provides both high performance and fault tolerance.

q: A full backup:
options: Copies only files changed since the last full backup
Copies only files changed since the last backup of any type
Creates a complete copy of all selected data
Backs up only the transaction log
explanation: A full backup creates a complete copy of all selected data (entire database or file set) at a point in time. It is self-contained and serves as the baseline for incremental and differential backups. It takes the most time and storage but provides the simplest restore.

q: An incremental backup copies:
options: All data since the last full backup
Only data changed since the last backup of ANY type (full or incremental)
Only data changed since the last full backup
The entire database every time
explanation: An incremental backup copies only the data that has changed since the last backup of any type â€” whether full or a previous incremental. This minimizes backup time and storage but requires the full backup plus ALL subsequent incrementals to restore.

q: A differential backup copies:
options: Only changes since the last backup of any type
All changes since the last FULL backup
A complete copy of all data
Only the database transaction log
explanation: A differential backup copies all data that has changed since the last FULL backup, regardless of any incremental backups taken in between. Restoration requires only the last full backup and the most recent differential â€” simpler than incremental but uses more storage.

q: Which backup strategy results in the FASTEST restore time when recovering from a full failure?
options: Full + incremental backups
Full + differential backups
Incremental-only backups
Differential-only backups
explanation: Full + differential requires restoring only 2 backup sets: the last full backup and the latest differential. Full + incremental requires restoring the full backup plus every incremental since, which takes longer. So full + differential offers faster restore at the cost of larger differential backups.

q: In the context of DBMS recovery, a 'fuzzy checkpoint' allows:
options: Checkpointing without stopping transaction processing
Checkpointing only when no transactions are active
Immediate rollback of all active transactions
Full backup to be created instantly
explanation: A fuzzy checkpoint allows the DBMS to write a checkpoint record without halting transaction processing. It records which transactions are active and which dirty pages exist at checkpoint time, allowing recovery to proceed without forcing a complete stop.

q: During recovery after a crash, transactions that were active (not committed) at the time of crash should be:
options: Redone
Ignored
Undone (rolled back)
Committed immediately
explanation: Transactions that were active (running) at the time of a crash did not commit, so their partial changes must be undone (rolled back) to restore the database to a consistent state. Only committed transactions are redone if their changes weren't flushed to disk.

q: The Log Sequence Number (LSN) in ARIES is used to:
options: Identify the size of the log file
Uniquely identify each log record and track the order of operations
Encrypt log records for security
Count the number of checkpoints
explanation: In ARIES, each log record is assigned a unique, monotonically increasing Log Sequence Number (LSN). LSNs are used to order log records, link records for the same transaction (via prevLSN), and track which updates have been applied to each page (pageLSN).

q: Which type of log record is written when a transaction issues a ROLLBACK?
options: Commit log record
Compensation Log Record (CLR)
Begin log record
Checkpoint log record
explanation: When a transaction is rolled back (either voluntarily or during recovery UNDO), Compensation Log Records (CLRs) are written to the log. CLRs record the undo operations performed and are themselves redo-only â€” they are never undone again.

q: In RAID 4, parity is:
options: Distributed across all disks
Stored on a single dedicated parity disk
Not used
Stored on every other disk
explanation: RAID 4 uses block-level striping with a single dedicated parity disk. All write operations must update the parity disk, creating a bottleneck. RAID 5 improves on this by distributing parity across all disks.

q: Which of the following statements about UNDO logging is correct?
options: UNDO logging writes after-images to the log
UNDO logging requires that the log record be written before the data page is written to disk, and the commit record written after data is flushed
UNDO logging does not require WAL
UNDO logging is used to redo committed transactions
explanation: UNDO logging rule: (1) write the log record (with before-image) before the data page is written to disk (WAL), and (2) write the COMMIT log record only after all dirty pages are written to disk. This allows UNDO of incomplete transactions but means all data must be flushed before commit.

q: Which of the following statements about REDO logging is correct?
options: REDO logging writes before-images to the log
REDO logging requires flushing data pages before the commit log record
REDO logging writes after-images and requires the commit log record to be written before data pages are flushed
REDO logging does not use the WAL principle
explanation: REDO logging rule: write after-images to the log, write the COMMIT record to the log before any data pages are flushed to disk. During recovery, committed transactions are redone using after-images. Uncommitted transactions are simply ignored (their data was never flushed).

q: UNDO/REDO logging (combined) requires which of the following?
options: Log records must be written after data pages are flushed
No WAL requirement is needed
Log record must be written before the corresponding data page is flushed (WAL), but no ordering constraint between commit and data flush
Data must be flushed before the transaction starts
explanation: UNDO/REDO logging combines both before-images and after-images. The only requirement is WAL â€” the log record must be written to stable storage before the data page is flushed. There is no constraint on when the commit record must be written relative to data page flushes.

q: The 'steal' buffer management policy means:
options: Buffer manager can write dirty pages of uncommitted transactions to disk
Buffer manager never writes uncommitted pages to disk
Only committed pages can be written to disk
Pages are stolen from other transactions' memory
explanation: The 'steal' policy allows the buffer manager to write (steal) dirty pages belonging to uncommitted transactions to disk when buffer space is needed. This requires UNDO capability. The opposite is 'no-steal', where uncommitted pages are never written to disk.

q: The 'force' buffer management policy means:
options: All dirty pages are written to disk at system startup
All dirty pages of a transaction are forced to disk at commit time
The buffer manager never forces writes
Force writes occur only during checkpoints
explanation: The 'force' policy requires that all dirty pages of a transaction be written to disk when the transaction commits. This eliminates the need for REDO during recovery. The 'no-force' policy (used in most real systems) allows pages to remain in buffer after commit, requiring REDO.

q: ARIES uses which combination of buffer management policies?
options: No-steal, Force
Steal, Force
No-steal, No-force
Steal, No-force
explanation: ARIES uses Steal + No-force: dirty pages of uncommitted transactions CAN be written to disk (steal), and committed pages are NOT forced to disk at commit time (no-force). This requires both UNDO (for stolen uncommitted pages) and REDO (for committed but not-yet-flushed pages).

q: Which RAID level provides NO fault tolerance but offers the best I/O performance by striping data across all disks?
options: RAID 1
RAID 5
RAID 0
RAID 6
explanation: RAID 0 stripes data across all disks with no parity or mirroring, delivering maximum read/write throughput. However, there is zero fault tolerance â€” failure of any single disk results in complete data loss.

q: During the Analysis phase of ARIES recovery, the system constructs:
options: A sorted list of all committed transactions
A Dirty Page Table (DPT) and a Transaction Table (TT) of active transactions at crash
A full copy of the database
A list of all checkpoints in the log
explanation: In the ARIES Analysis phase, the system scans the log from the last checkpoint forward to reconstruct the Dirty Page Table (pages that may not have been flushed) and the Transaction Table (transactions active at crash time). This information guides the Redo and Undo phases.

q: In the context of backup, RTO (Recovery Time Objective) refers to:
options: The maximum amount of data loss an organization can tolerate
The maximum acceptable time to restore systems after a failure
The frequency of full backups
The size of the backup storage
explanation: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum acceptable downtime â€” the time within which systems must be restored after a failure. It drives decisions about backup frequency and restore procedures. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) addresses acceptable data loss.

q: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) refers to:
options: The time taken to perform a backup
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time
The physical location of backup storage
The number of backup copies maintained
explanation: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines the maximum tolerable period of data loss â€” i.e., how far back in time the database can be restored. A lower RPO requires more frequent backups or continuous log shipping.

q: Which technique involves shipping transaction log files to a standby database server to maintain synchronization?
options: Full backup
Differential backup
Log shipping
Shadow paging
explanation: Log shipping involves periodically copying (shipping) transaction log backups from a primary database server to one or more standby servers, then restoring them. This keeps the standby in sync and allows failover with minimal data loss.

q: In a B-tree index, after a system crash, what ensures the index remains consistent with the data?
options: RAID mirroring
Write-ahead logging of all index modifications
Full backup taken before crash
Shadow paging of index pages only
explanation: Write-ahead logging (WAL) is applied to index modifications just as to data modifications. All changes to B-tree index pages are logged before being applied to disk, ensuring that REDO/UNDO during recovery keeps the index consistent with the database.

q: Which of the following recovery scenarios requires ONLY REDO and NOT UNDO?
options: Immediate modification with no-force policy after a crash with active transactions
Deferred modification where all active transactions had committed before the crash but pages were not flushed
Crash with some transactions in the middle of execution
Steal buffer policy with uncommitted active transactions
explanation: In deferred modification, uncommitted changes never reach the disk. If all active transactions committed before the crash but some pages weren't flushed (no-force), only REDO is needed to reapply the committed but unflushed changes. No UNDO is needed since no uncommitted data is on disk.

q: A 'hot backup' (online backup) is one that is taken:
options: Only when the database is shut down
While the database is running and fully accessible to users
Only during maintenance windows
After a crash to capture the final state
explanation: A hot backup (online backup) is taken while the database is running and serving user queries. It requires special mechanisms (like archivelog mode in Oracle) to ensure consistency. A 'cold backup' (offline backup) is taken with the database shut down.

q: Which RAID level is commonly used for databases requiring both high performance and fault tolerance, using distributed parity?
options: RAID 0
RAID 1
RAID 5
RAID 2
explanation: RAID 5 (block-level striping with distributed parity) is widely used for database storage because it balances read performance, fault tolerance (single disk failure), and storage efficiency. It distributes parity across all disks, avoiding the single-parity-disk bottleneck of RAID 4.

q: The 'pageLSN' field stored in each database page is used in ARIES to:
options: Encrypt page content
Identify the LSN of the most recent log record that updated the page, used during REDO to skip already-applied updates
Count transactions that accessed the page
Store the page's creation timestamp
explanation: In ARIES, each page stores a pageLSN â€” the LSN of the most recent log record that modified that page. During the REDO phase, if a log record's LSN â‰¤ pageLSN, the update was already applied before the crash and can be safely skipped (idempotent REDO).

q: Which of the following correctly describes 'media failure' in database recovery?
options: A transaction voluntarily rolling back
Physical damage to storage media (disk crash) that destroys data permanently
A deadlock between two transactions
Buffer overflow in main memory
explanation: Media failure refers to physical damage to storage devices (e.g., disk head crash, sector corruption) that causes permanent, irreversible loss of data on disk. Recovery from media failure requires restoring from backup and replaying archived logs â€” cannot be fixed by log-based recovery alone.

q: In shadow paging, recovery from a transaction abort is achieved by:
options: Scanning the log and applying UNDO records
Discarding the current page table and reverting to the shadow page table
Writing checkpoint records to disk
Replaying all transactions from the beginning of the log
explanation: In shadow paging, when a transaction aborts, recovery is simple: discard the current page table (which reflects uncommitted changes) and revert to the shadow page table (which holds the last committed state). No log scanning is needed for abort recovery.

q: The 'no-undo/redo' (deferred update, no-steal/force) approach to recovery means:
options: No logging is needed
Changes are never written to disk during transaction, and all changes are forced to disk at commit â€” no UNDO or REDO needed for recovery
UNDO is needed but REDO is not
REDO is needed but UNDO is not
explanation: With no-steal (uncommitted changes never on disk) + force (all committed changes flushed at commit), no UNDO is needed (nothing uncommitted on disk) and no REDO is needed (all committed data already on disk). However, this approach restricts buffer management flexibility.

q: Which of the following is a key advantage of incremental backup over full backup?
options: Faster restore time
Less storage space and shorter backup window since only changed data is backed up
No dependency on previous backups
Better fault tolerance
explanation: Incremental backups use significantly less storage and take less time than full backups because they only copy data that changed since the last backup. The trade-off is that restoring requires the full backup plus all subsequent incrementals, increasing restore time.

q: RAID 2 uses which type of error correction?
options: Simple XOR parity
Hamming code for error correction and detection
Mirroring
No error correction
explanation: RAID 2 uses Hamming code for error correction and detection, with bit-level striping across data disks and multiple dedicated error-correction disks. It is rarely used in practice because modern disk drives already include built-in error correction, making RAID 2's overhead unnecessary.

q: During recovery, the 'Redo phase' in ARIES replays history starting from:
options: The very beginning of the log
The beginning of the oldest active transaction in the log
The smallest LSN in the Dirty Page Table (redoLSN)
The last COMMIT record
explanation: In ARIES, the Redo phase starts from the smallest recLSN (recovery LSN) found in the Dirty Page Table â€” the point from which dirty pages may not have been flushed. This is the redoLSN. History is replayed forward from this point to reconstruct the database state at crash time.

q: Which backup type is most suitable when you need to minimize both backup storage space and restore time in a balanced way?
options: Full backup only
Incremental backup only
Differential backup with a periodic full backup
No backup
explanation: Differential backups offer a good balance: they use less storage than full backups (only changes since last full) and restore faster than incremental (only 2 sets needed: last full + latest differential). For minimal storage, incrementals are better; for fastest restore, differentials are better.

q: In a transaction log, the record '<T1, X, 50, 100>' using UNDO/REDO logging means:
options: Transaction T1 read value 50 and wrote value 100 to X
Transaction T1 updated data item X from old value 50 to new value 100
Transaction T1 created data item X with initial value 50 and final value 100
Transaction T1 deleted X having value 50 and inserted X with value 100
explanation: In standard UNDO/REDO log notation, <Ti, X, old_value, new_value> means transaction Ti updated data item X from its old value (before-image = 50) to its new value (after-image = 100). Old value is used for UNDO; new value is used for REDO.

q: Which type of view in SQL stores the query result physically on disk and can be refreshed periodically?
options: Simple view
Complex view
Materialized view
Updatable view
explanation: A materialized view physically stores the query result on disk (unlike regular views which store only the query definition). It improves query performance but requires periodic refresh to stay synchronized with base tables.

q: A simple view in SQL is defined as a view that:
options: Contains GROUP BY and aggregate functions
Is based on a single table with no aggregate functions or GROUP BY
Always allows INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations
Stores data physically like a table
explanation: A simple view is derived from a single base table, contains no aggregate functions, GROUP BY, DISTINCT, or subqueries. It typically supports DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) since each row maps directly to a base table row.

q: Which of the following views CANNOT be updated (non-updatable) in standard SQL?
options: A view based on a single table selecting all columns
A view with a WHERE clause but no aggregates
A view containing a GROUP BY clause with aggregate functions
A view that is a simple projection of a single table
explanation: Views containing GROUP BY, HAVING, or aggregate functions are non-updatable because a single row in the view corresponds to multiple rows in the base table, making it impossible to determine which base rows to modify.

q: The WITH CHECK OPTION clause in a view definition ensures that:
options: The view is read-only and no DML is allowed
Any INSERT or UPDATE through the view must satisfy the view's WHERE condition
The view is automatically refreshed when base table changes
Indexes are created on the view automatically
explanation: WITH CHECK OPTION ensures that any INSERT or UPDATE performed through the view must satisfy the view's WHERE clause predicate. If the modified row would not be visible through the view, the operation is rejected.

q: A complex view in SQL differs from a simple view primarily because it:
options: Is stored physically on disk
Can involve multiple tables, joins, aggregates, or subqueries
Always has better performance than simple views
Cannot be queried like a table
explanation: A complex view can involve joins across multiple tables, aggregate functions, GROUP BY, DISTINCT, subqueries, or set operations. Complex views are generally non-updatable due to the ambiguity in mapping view rows back to base table rows.

q: To refresh a materialized view in Oracle SQL, which command is used?
options: ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REBUILD
EXEC DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH(...)
UPDATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ...
REFRESH VIEW ...
explanation: In Oracle, materialized views are refreshed using the DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH procedure (e.g., EXEC DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH('view_name')). The refresh can be COMPLETE (full recomputation) or FAST (incremental using materialized view logs).

q: Which of the following is TRUE about stored procedures in RDBMS?
options: Stored procedures are compiled and stored in the database server
Stored procedures cannot accept parameters
Stored procedures are executed on the client side
Stored procedures cannot contain control flow statements like IF or LOOP
explanation: Stored procedures are precompiled SQL code blocks stored in the database server. They accept IN, OUT, and INOUT parameters, support control flow (IF, LOOP, WHILE), and reduce network traffic by executing logic on the server side.

q: What is the primary advantage of using stored procedures over ad-hoc SQL queries?
options: Stored procedures always run faster than any SQL query
They reduce network overhead and allow code reuse with precompilation
They prevent all SQL injection attacks without any additional measures
They can only be called from within the database, not from applications
explanation: Stored procedures reduce network traffic (only the procedure call is sent, not the full SQL), allow code reuse across multiple applications, and benefit from precompilation/caching. They also support encapsulation and security (granting EXECUTE privilege instead of table access).

q: In SQL, a FUNCTION differs from a STORED PROCEDURE primarily in that:
options: Functions cannot have parameters but procedures can
Functions must return a value and can be used in SQL expressions; procedures may not return a value
Procedures are faster than functions
Functions support transactions but procedures do not
explanation: A SQL function must return a single value (scalar or table) and can be embedded directly in SQL expressions (SELECT, WHERE). A stored procedure uses OUT parameters to return values and cannot be used directly in SQL expressions.

q: Which SQL statement is used to create a stored procedure in standard SQL/MySQL?
options: CREATE FUNCTION procedure_name AS ...
DEFINE PROCEDURE procedure_name BEGIN ... END
CREATE PROCEDURE procedure_name() BEGIN ... END
STORE PROCEDURE procedure_name AS BEGIN ... END
explanation: The standard syntax to create a stored procedure is: CREATE PROCEDURE procedure_name(parameters) BEGIN ... END; The body contains SQL statements, variables, control flow, and can include DML and DDL statements depending on the RDBMS.

q: A TRIGGER in a database is defined as:
options: A stored procedure executed manually by the DBA
A special procedure that automatically executes in response to specific DML events on a table
A constraint that prevents invalid data insertion
An index structure that triggers query optimization
explanation: A trigger is a database object that automatically fires (executes) in response to DML events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) or DDL events on a table or view. Triggers enforce business rules, maintain audit trails, and synchronize data without explicit application code.

q: A BEFORE trigger in SQL fires:
options: After the DML statement has completed and data is committed
Before the triggering DML statement executes, allowing modification of new values
Only during SELECT operations on the table
After a COMMIT statement is issued
explanation: A BEFORE trigger fires before the triggering DML operation executes. This allows the trigger to validate or modify the :NEW values before they are written to the table. It is commonly used for data validation and value transformation.

q: An AFTER trigger is most commonly used for:
options: Preventing invalid data from being inserted
Modifying the NEW row values before insertion
Auditing changes, updating related tables, or sending notifications after a DML operation completes
Rolling back transactions automatically
explanation: AFTER triggers fire after the DML operation completes. They are ideal for audit logging (recording old/new values), cascading updates to related tables, or triggering notifications, since the operation has already succeeded at this point.

q: A ROW-LEVEL trigger in SQL fires:
options: Once per DML statement regardless of rows affected
Once for each row affected by the triggering DML statement
Only when the entire table is locked
Only for DDL statements like CREATE or DROP
explanation: A row-level trigger (FOR EACH ROW) fires once for every row affected by the triggering DML statement. If an UPDATE modifies 100 rows, a row-level trigger fires 100 times. It has access to :OLD and :NEW pseudorecords for each row.

q: A STATEMENT-LEVEL trigger fires:
options: Once for each row modified by the DML statement
Once per DML statement, regardless of how many rows are affected
Only for single-row operations
Twice â€” once before and once after each row change
explanation: A statement-level trigger fires once per DML statement, regardless of the number of rows affected. Even if a DELETE removes 1000 rows, a statement-level trigger fires only once. It does not have access to individual :OLD/:NEW row values.

q: In Oracle PL/SQL, the :NEW and :OLD pseudorecords in a trigger refer to:
options: :NEW is the row before the change; :OLD is the row after the change
:NEW is the updated/inserted row values; :OLD is the original row values before the change
Both refer to the same current row being processed
:NEW and :OLD are only available in statement-level triggers
explanation: :NEW contains the new values of the row being inserted or updated; :OLD contains the original values before the update or delete. For INSERT, :OLD is NULL; for DELETE, :NEW is NULL. These pseudorecords are available only in row-level triggers.

q: Which of the following is a valid use case for a database trigger?
options: Replacing stored procedures for better performance
Automatically maintaining an audit log of all changes to a sensitive table
Speeding up SELECT queries on large tables
Replacing foreign key constraints
explanation: Triggers are well-suited for maintaining audit logs that automatically record who changed what data and when. They execute transparently without requiring application-level code changes, ensuring all changes are captured regardless of how the data is modified.

q: What is a database CURSOR used for?
options: To point to the current position in a B-tree index
To process a result set row-by-row in procedural code (stored procedures, triggers)
To lock a table during concurrent access
To cache query execution plans
explanation: A cursor is a database object that allows row-by-row processing of a query result set within procedural code (PL/SQL, T-SQL). The cursor lifecycle involves DECLARE, OPEN, FETCH (one row at a time), and CLOSE operations.

q: The correct order of cursor operations in PL/SQL is:
options: OPEN â†’ DECLARE â†’ FETCH â†’ CLOSE
DECLARE â†’ OPEN â†’ FETCH â†’ CLOSE
FETCH â†’ DECLARE â†’ OPEN â†’ CLOSE
DECLARE â†’ FETCH â†’ OPEN â†’ CLOSE
explanation: The standard cursor lifecycle is: DECLARE (define the cursor with its SELECT query) â†’ OPEN (execute the query and create the result set) â†’ FETCH (retrieve one row at a time) â†’ CLOSE (release the cursor resources). CLOSE should always be called to free memory.

q: An IMPLICIT cursor in Oracle PL/SQL is:
options: A cursor explicitly declared using CURSOR keyword
Automatically created by Oracle for every DML statement and single-row SELECT INTO
A cursor that must be manually opened and closed
A cursor used only for stored procedures
explanation: Oracle automatically creates an implicit cursor for every DML statement (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and SELECT INTO statement. The most recent implicit cursor is accessed via SQL% attributes: SQL%ROWCOUNT, SQL%FOUND, SQL%NOTFOUND, SQL%ISOPEN.

q: A CURSOR FOR LOOP in PL/SQL is preferred because it:
options: Provides faster execution than explicit cursors in all scenarios
Automatically handles OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE operations, reducing code and risk of resource leaks
Can process multiple cursors simultaneously
Allows modification of the cursor's SELECT statement during iteration
explanation: A cursor FOR LOOP automatically opens the cursor, fetches each row into a loop variable, and closes the cursor when done. This reduces boilerplate code and eliminates the risk of forgetting to close the cursor, which would cause resource leaks.

q: Query optimization in a DBMS primarily aims to:
options: Minimize disk space used by tables
Find the execution plan with minimum estimated cost (I/O, CPU, memory)
Maximize the number of indexes on a table
Ensure all queries use sequential scans for consistency
explanation: The query optimizer's goal is to find the most efficient execution plan by estimating and minimizing cost (typically measured in I/O operations, CPU usage, and memory). It evaluates multiple equivalent plans and chooses the one with the lowest estimated cost.

q: A query execution plan (query plan) in RDBMS describes:
options: The list of indexes available on all tables
The step-by-step operations the database engine will use to execute a query
The physical storage layout of tables on disk
The network topology used for distributed query execution
explanation: A query execution plan is a tree of physical operators (e.g., Index Scan, Hash Join, Sort, Aggregate) that the database engine will execute to produce query results. It can be inspected using EXPLAIN (MySQL/PostgreSQL) or EXPLAIN PLAN (Oracle).

q: In query optimization, cost estimation uses which of the following statistics?
options: Number of active database connections
Cardinality (number of rows), selectivity, and histogram data about column value distributions
The physical memory size of the database server
The number of stored procedures in the database
explanation: Cost estimation relies on statistics like: cardinality (estimated row counts), selectivity (fraction of rows satisfying a predicate), column histograms (value distributions), and index statistics. These help the optimizer estimate the cost of different access paths and join methods.

q: In SQL query optimization, 'join ordering' refers to:
options: Sorting the result of a join by specified columns
Determining the sequence in which tables are joined to minimize intermediate result sizes
Creating indexes on join columns automatically
Replacing nested-loop joins with hash joins
explanation: Join ordering determines the sequence in which tables are joined. Since join is not associative in terms of cost, different orderings produce different intermediate result sizes. The optimizer uses dynamic programming or heuristics to find the ordering that minimizes total intermediate data.

q: Which join algorithm is typically most efficient for joining a large table with a small table where the small table fits in memory?
options: Nested-loop join
Merge join
Hash join
Cross join
explanation: A hash join builds a hash table from the smaller (build) relation in memory, then probes it with each row from the larger (probe) relation. When the build side fits in memory, hash join is very efficient (O(N+M) complexity). It is the optimizer's default choice for large unsorted tables.

q: Query rewriting in SQL optimization refers to:
options: Manually rewriting SQL queries for better readability
The optimizer automatically transforming a query into a semantically equivalent form that executes more efficiently
Replacing stored procedures with inline SQL
Converting SQL queries to NoSQL queries
explanation: Query rewriting is a transformation phase where the optimizer converts a logical query into a semantically equivalent form that is cheaper to execute. Examples include: subquery flattening, predicate pushdown, view merging, constant folding, and eliminating redundant joins.

q: Predicate pushdown in query optimization means:
options: Adding more WHERE conditions to a query
Moving filter conditions as close to the base table scan as possible to reduce intermediate result sizes
Pushing predicates into stored procedures
Using index-based access instead of sequential scans
explanation: Predicate pushdown (or selection pushdown) moves WHERE clause conditions down the query plan tree to be applied earlier, reducing the number of rows processed at higher levels. For example, filtering before a join rather than after reduces join input size significantly.

q: The EXPLAIN command in MySQL/PostgreSQL is used to:
options: Execute a query and explain each result row
Show the query execution plan chosen by the optimizer without actually executing the query
Create an explanation of table structures
Optimize a query automatically and rewrite it
explanation: EXPLAIN shows the query execution plan (access methods, join types, estimated rows, costs) chosen by the optimizer without running the query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE (PostgreSQL) or EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON (MySQL) actually executes the query and shows actual vs. estimated statistics.

q: NoSQL databases differ from RDBMS primarily in that NoSQL databases:
options: Always use SQL as the query language
Provide flexible schemas, horizontal scalability, and are optimized for specific data models (document, key-value, column-family, graph)
Guarantee full ACID transactions across all operations
Cannot store large datasets
explanation: NoSQL databases use flexible (schema-less or schema-optional) data models, are designed for horizontal scaling across commodity servers, and are optimized for specific access patterns. They trade some ACID guarantees for availability and partition tolerance (per CAP theorem).

q: Which of the following is a key-value NoSQL database?
options: MongoDB
Cassandra
Redis
Neo4j
explanation: Redis is a key-value store where data is accessed by a unique key, making it extremely fast for caching and session management. MongoDB is a document store, Cassandra is a column-family (wide-column) store, and Neo4j is a graph database.

q: MongoDB is classified as which type of NoSQL database?
options: Key-value store
Document store
Column-family store
Graph database
explanation: MongoDB is a document-oriented NoSQL database that stores data as JSON-like BSON documents. Each document can have a different structure (flexible schema). It supports rich queries, indexing, and aggregation pipelines on nested document data.

q: Apache Cassandra is best classified as a:
options: Relational database
Document store
Wide-column (column-family) NoSQL store
Graph database
explanation: Cassandra is a wide-column store where data is organized by rows and dynamic columns grouped into column families. It is designed for high write throughput, linear horizontal scalability, and high availability with no single point of failure using a peer-to-peer architecture.

q: The CAP theorem, proposed by Eric Brewer, states that a distributed system can guarantee at most how many of the three properties simultaneously?
options: All three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance
Two of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance
Only one: either Consistency or Availability
None â€” all three are always unavailable in distributed systems
explanation: CAP theorem states that in the presence of a network partition, a distributed system can provide either Consistency (all nodes see the same data) or Availability (every request receives a response), but not both simultaneously. Since network partitions are unavoidable, systems must choose between CP or AP.

q: In the CAP theorem, 'Consistency' means:
options: Data is never lost even after system failures
Every read receives the most recent write or an error (all nodes have the same data at the same time)
The system is always available for read and write operations
The system can handle network partitions without data loss
explanation: CAP Consistency (not ACID consistency) means every read from any node returns the most recent committed write. All nodes in the distributed system reflect the same data at any given time. This is also known as linearizability or strong consistency.

q: In the CAP theorem, 'Availability' means:
options: Data is consistent across all nodes
Every request to the system receives a non-error response (though it may not be the most recent data)
The system can tolerate network partitions
Only read operations are guaranteed to succeed
explanation: CAP Availability means every request (read or write) to a non-failing node receives a response â€” though the response may not contain the most recently written data. The system remains operational even during partial failures, prioritizing uptime over strict correctness.

q: In the CAP theorem, 'Partition Tolerance' means:
options: The system can split data across multiple servers
The system continues to operate even when network messages between nodes are dropped or delayed
The system partitions data for better query performance
Transactions are partitioned into sub-transactions for efficiency
explanation: Partition Tolerance means the system continues to function even when network communication between nodes fails (messages are lost or delayed â€” a network partition). Since partitions are an unavoidable reality in distributed systems, P is generally considered mandatory, making the real choice between C and A.

q: A system that prioritizes Consistency and Partition Tolerance (CP) will:
options: Return stale data during a network partition rather than becoming unavailable
Become unavailable (return an error) during a network partition rather than return potentially stale data
Always guarantee both consistency and availability
Sacrifice partition tolerance when consistency is needed
explanation: A CP system (e.g., HBase, Zookeeper, MongoDB in certain configs) chooses to refuse requests (become unavailable) during a network partition rather than risk returning inconsistent/stale data. It prioritizes data correctness over availability.

q: Which of the following databases is an example of an AP system (Available + Partition Tolerant) per CAP theorem?
options: HBase
Zookeeper
Cassandra
MySQL with ACID transactions
explanation: Cassandra is an AP system â€” it remains available during network partitions but may return stale data (eventual consistency). HBase and Zookeeper are CP systems that sacrifice availability for consistency. Traditional RDBMS like MySQL prioritize CA but lack partition tolerance in distributed setups.

q: The BASE properties in NoSQL databases stand for:
options: Basic Atomicity, Simplified Execution
Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency
Binary Access, Structured Encoding
Batch Atomicity, Sequential Execution
explanation: BASE stands for: Basically Available (system guarantees availability), Soft state (state may change over time even without input due to eventual consistency), and Eventually consistent (system will eventually reach a consistent state given no new inputs). BASE is the NoSQL alternative to ACID.

q: The 'Basically Available' property in BASE means:
options: The system is available only during business hours
The system guarantees availability but may return stale or partial data
The system uses basic SQL for availability checks
Only basic read operations are available at all times
explanation: Basically Available means the system guarantees some response to every request, but the response may be stale, partial, or indicate temporary unavailability of part of the data. The system appears available even during partial failures or replication lag.

q: The 'Soft State' property in BASE refers to:
options: Data is stored in soft (RAM) storage only
The system state may change over time even without new input, due to eventual consistency propagation
Database connections are soft-closed after each query
Indexes are rebuilt periodically (soft rebuild)
explanation: Soft state means the system's state can change over time even without new writes, as updates propagate asynchronously across nodes. Unlike ACID's durable, hard state, a BASE system's intermediate states may be inconsistent as replicas converge toward consistency.

q: The 'Eventual Consistency' property in BASE guarantees that:
options: All reads return the latest write immediately
If no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same consistent state
Data is consistent only at transaction boundaries
The system never serves stale data
explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new updates are applied to a data item, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. Reads may temporarily return stale data during propagation, but the system will become consistent over time. This is the foundation of Dynamo, Cassandra, and CouchDB.

q: Which of the following best describes the fundamental difference between ACID and BASE?
options: ACID is for NoSQL and BASE is for RDBMS
ACID prioritizes strong consistency and correctness; BASE prioritizes availability and scalability with relaxed consistency
ACID systems use horizontal scaling; BASE systems use vertical scaling
There is no difference â€” both guarantee the same properties
explanation: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) prioritizes strict correctness and consistency, suitable for financial and transactional systems. BASE relaxes consistency guarantees in favor of high availability and horizontal scalability, suitable for large-scale distributed systems like social media and e-commerce catalogs.

q: In a view, the term 'view materialization' refers to:
options: Creating a physical copy of the view's result set stored on disk
Converting a virtual view into a stored procedure
Adding materialized indexes to the base tables of a view
Granting materialized (permanent) access privileges to a view
explanation: View materialization means physically storing the result of the view query on disk (as a materialized view). Unlike virtual views (which re-execute the query each time), materialized views store precomputed results, improving read performance at the cost of additional storage and refresh overhead.

q: Which SQL command is used to remove a view from the database?
options: DELETE VIEW view_name
REMOVE VIEW view_name
DROP VIEW view_name
TRUNCATE VIEW view_name
explanation: DROP VIEW view_name removes the view definition from the database catalog. It does not affect the underlying base tables. If CASCADE is specified, dependent objects (other views built on this view) are also dropped.

q: Which of the following statements about triggers is INCORRECT?
options: Triggers can be used to enforce referential integrity
A table can have multiple triggers for the same event
Triggers can directly be called by application code using EXEC or CALL
Triggers automatically execute in response to DML events
explanation: Triggers cannot be called directly by application code â€” they fire automatically in response to DML events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). Stored procedures can be called explicitly, but triggers are implicitly invoked by the database engine when the triggering event occurs.

q: In a stored procedure, the INOUT parameter mode means:
options: The parameter can only pass values into the procedure
The parameter can only return values from the procedure
The parameter both receives an input value and returns a modified output value
The parameter is optional and has a default value
explanation: In SQL stored procedures, parameters have three modes: IN (passes value into procedure, default), OUT (returns value from procedure), and INOUT (both receives an initial value AND returns a modified value). INOUT allows bidirectional data flow for a single parameter.

q: The MERGE join algorithm in query optimization requires that:
options: Both input relations are already sorted on the join key
One relation fits entirely in memory
Both relations have hash indexes on the join key
A cross product is computed first
explanation: Sort-Merge join requires both input relations to be sorted on the join attribute. If not already sorted, the optimizer must add a Sort operator, which may make it costlier than Hash join. However, for pre-sorted or indexed data, merge join is very efficient (O(N+M)) and produces sorted output.

q: In query optimization, a 'dense index' is one where:
options: The index contains one entry for every record in the data file
The index contains one entry for each unique block of the data file
The index is stored in compressed (dense) format
Multiple columns are indexed together in a composite index
explanation: A dense index has one index entry for every record (row) in the data file. A sparse index has entries only for some records (typically one per data block). Dense indexes allow direct access to any record but require more space; sparse indexes are smaller but may require additional I/O.

q: Which NoSQL data model is most appropriate for storing and traversing relationships between entities, such as social network connections?
options: Key-value store
Document store
Column-family store
Graph database
explanation: Graph databases (e.g., Neo4j) are optimized for storing entities (nodes) and relationships (edges) between them. They excel at traversal queries (friend-of-friend, shortest path, recommendations) that would require expensive multi-level JOINs in an RDBMS.

q: In the context of materialized views, a 'FAST REFRESH' means:
options: The entire view is recomputed from scratch very quickly
Only the changes (deltas) since the last refresh are applied to the materialized view
The view is refreshed in real-time with every DML change
The refresh is triggered by a fast timer of 1 second
explanation: A FAST (incremental) refresh applies only the changes (inserts, updates, deletes) since the last refresh using a materialized view log (change log on the base table). This is much faster than COMPLETE refresh (full recomputation) for large datasets with few changes.

q: Which of the following scenarios would benefit MOST from replacing a correlated subquery with a JOIN in query optimization (query rewriting)?
options: When the subquery references no columns from the outer query
When the correlated subquery is executed once per row of the outer query, causing O(NÂ²) complexity
When the subquery contains GROUP BY and aggregate functions
When the subquery is used in a HAVING clause
explanation: A correlated subquery re-executes for each row of the outer query, leading to O(N*M) complexity. Rewriting it as a JOIN allows the optimizer to choose efficient join algorithms (hash join, merge join) with O(N+M) or O(N*logM) complexity, dramatically improving performance for large tables.

q: In distributed databases, horizontal fragmentation divides a relation by:
options: Splitting columns into subsets
Splitting rows based on a selection condition
Combining rows and columns together
Replicating data across all sites
explanation: Horizontal fragmentation splits a relation into subsets of tuples (rows) based on a selection predicate, so each fragment contains a subset of rows satisfying a condition. Vertical fragmentation splits by columns.

q: Which type of fragmentation in distributed databases involves dividing a table both by rows and by columns?
options: Horizontal fragmentation
Vertical fragmentation
Mixed (hybrid) fragmentation
Derived fragmentation
explanation: Mixed (hybrid) fragmentation combines horizontal and vertical fragmentation â€” the table is first fragmented one way, then the resulting fragments are further fragmented the other way, dividing both rows and columns.

q: In vertical fragmentation, each fragment must retain the ______ of the original relation to allow reconstruction.
options: Foreign key
Primary key
Candidate key
Composite key
explanation: In vertical fragmentation, each fragment must include the primary key of the original relation so that the full relation can be reconstructed by joining the fragments on the primary key.

q: Which property of fragmentation ensures that no tuple or attribute is lost and the original relation can be reconstructed?
options: Disjointness
Completeness
Reconstruction
Allocation
explanation: The reconstruction property guarantees that fragments can be combined (via UNION for horizontal, JOIN for vertical) to reconstruct the original relation with no data loss. Completeness ensures all data belongs to some fragment; Disjointness ensures no overlap (for horizontal).

q: Replication in distributed databases refers to:
options: Splitting the database into fragments
Storing copies of data at multiple sites
Encrypting data across sites
Compressing data for remote access
explanation: Replication stores copies of the same data (full or partial) at multiple sites. It improves availability and read performance but increases update complexity since all replicas must be kept consistent.

q: In a fully replicated distributed database, the database is stored:
options: At exactly two sites only
At the primary site only
At every site in the distributed system
Only at sites that request the data
explanation: In a fully replicated distributed database, a complete copy of the entire database is stored at every participating site, maximizing availability and read performance but making writes expensive.

q: A distributed transaction that spans multiple sites requires which protocol to ensure atomicity?
options: One-phase commit
Two-phase commit (2PC)
Three-phase locking
Timestamp ordering
explanation: The Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol ensures atomicity for distributed transactions spanning multiple sites. In Phase 1 (voting), the coordinator asks all participants to vote commit or abort. In Phase 2 (decision), the coordinator broadcasts the final decision.

q: In the Two-Phase Commit protocol, what happens in Phase 1?
options: The coordinator sends a COMMIT message to all participants
The coordinator sends PREPARE messages and collects votes from participants
Participants independently decide to commit or abort
The coordinator rolls back if any participant is unavailable
explanation: Phase 1 (Prepare/Voting phase) of 2PC: The coordinator sends PREPARE messages to all participants. Each participant votes YES (ready to commit) or NO (abort). If all vote YES, the coordinator decides COMMIT; otherwise ABORT.

q: A major drawback of the Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol is:
options: It cannot guarantee atomicity
It may block indefinitely if the coordinator fails after Phase 1
It requires three rounds of communication
It only works with two participating sites
explanation: 2PC is a blocking protocol â€” if the coordinator crashes after sending PREPARE but before sending the final decision, participants that voted YES are blocked (they cannot commit or abort unilaterally) until the coordinator recovers.

q: Which of the following is NOT a transparency goal in distributed databases?
options: Location transparency
Replication transparency
Fragmentation transparency
Encryption transparency
explanation: The standard distribution transparencies are location transparency (hide physical location), replication transparency (hide data copies), and fragmentation transparency (hide how data is split). Encryption transparency is a security concept, not a standard distributed DB transparency.

q: Object-oriented databases store data as:
options: Two-dimensional tables with rows and columns
Objects with attributes and methods
XML documents with hierarchical structure
Key-value pairs only
explanation: Object-oriented databases (OODBs) store data as objects, similar to OOP. Each object has attributes (data) and methods (behavior), and supports concepts like inheritance, encapsulation, and polymorphism.

q: Which concept in object-oriented databases allows a subclass to inherit attributes and methods from a superclass?
options: Encapsulation
Polymorphism
Inheritance
Aggregation
explanation: Inheritance in OODBs allows a subclass (child class) to inherit all attributes and methods of its superclass (parent class), enabling code reuse and hierarchical classification of objects.

q: The Object Query Language (OQL) is associated with which type of database?
options: Relational databases
Object-oriented databases
XML databases
NoSQL key-value stores
explanation: OQL (Object Query Language) is a query language defined by the Object Data Management Group (ODMG) for object-oriented databases. It is similar to SQL but designed for querying object databases.

q: In object-oriented databases, the concept of hiding the internal implementation of an object from external access is called:
options: Inheritance
Polymorphism
Encapsulation
Abstraction
explanation: Encapsulation in OODBs means that the internal state (attributes) of an object is hidden from external access; the object is only accessible through its defined methods (interface), protecting data integrity.

q: Which XML feature allows defining the structure, elements, and attributes that an XML document must conform to?
options: XPath
XSLT
DTD (Document Type Definition)
XQuery
explanation: A DTD (Document Type Definition) specifies the structure (allowed elements, attributes, and their arrangement) that an XML document must follow. XML Schema (XSD) is a more powerful alternative to DTD.

q: XPath is used in XML databases primarily for:
options: Transforming XML documents into HTML
Navigating and querying elements within an XML document
Defining the schema of an XML document
Encrypting XML data
explanation: XPath is a language for navigating the tree structure of XML documents and selecting nodes (elements, attributes, text). It is used within XQuery and XSLT to locate specific parts of an XML document.

q: XQuery is to XML databases what SQL is to:
options: Object-oriented databases
Relational databases
NoSQL databases
Hierarchical databases
explanation: XQuery is the standard query language for XML databases, just as SQL is the standard query language for relational databases. XQuery uses XPath expressions and supports FLWOR expressions (For, Let, Where, Order by, Return).

q: XSLT (Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations) is used to:
options: Query XML data using XPath
Transform XML documents into other formats (HTML, text, another XML)
Define XML schema constraints
Store XML documents in a relational database
explanation: XSLT is a language for transforming XML documents into other formats such as HTML, plain text, or differently structured XML. It uses templates that match XML elements and specifies how to transform them.

q: The data dictionary (system catalog) in a DBMS stores:
options: Actual user data
Metadata about the database structure (tables, columns, indexes, etc.)
Transaction logs only
Backup copies of database objects
explanation: The data dictionary (system catalog) stores metadata â€” information about the database itself: table names, column names and types, constraints, indexes, views, stored procedures, user privileges, and other structural information.

q: Which of the following is typically NOT stored in a DBMS system catalog?
options: Table and column names
User access privileges
Index definitions
The actual row data of user tables
explanation: The system catalog stores metadata (structural information) like table definitions, column types, constraints, indexes, and privileges. Actual user/row data is stored in the data files, not in the system catalog.

q: The system catalog is also known as:
options: Data warehouse
Data dictionary
Transaction log
Buffer pool
explanation: The system catalog is also commonly called the data dictionary. It is a set of system tables maintained by the DBMS that contains metadata describing all database objects, users, and privileges.

q: In SQL injection attacks, an attacker typically exploits:
options: Weak encryption algorithms
Insufficient input validation that allows malicious SQL code in user inputs
Buffer overflow in database memory
Weak hashing of passwords
explanation: SQL injection occurs when user input is not properly validated/sanitized and is directly embedded in SQL queries. An attacker can insert malicious SQL code (e.g., ' OR '1'='1) to manipulate queries, bypass authentication, or access unauthorized data.

q: Which of the following is the BEST defense against SQL injection attacks?
options: Using stored procedures only
Parameterized queries (prepared statements)
Encrypting the database
Using a firewall
explanation: Parameterized queries (prepared statements) are the most effective defense against SQL injection. They separate SQL code from data â€” user input is treated as a parameter value, not as executable SQL code, preventing injection.

q: Consider the login query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='x'. This is an example of:
options: Privilege escalation
SQL injection
Cross-site scripting
Buffer overflow
explanation: This is a classic SQL injection attack. The input ' OR '1'='1' -- makes the WHERE clause always true and comments out the password check, allowing the attacker to log in without a valid password.

q: In database security, the GRANT statement is used to:
options: Remove user privileges
Assign privileges to users or roles
Audit user activity
Create database backups
explanation: The GRANT statement in SQL is used to give specific privileges (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, etc.) on database objects to users or roles. Its counterpart REVOKE removes privileges.

q: The REVOKE statement in SQL is used to:
options: Grant new privileges to a user
Remove previously granted privileges from a user
Create a new role in the database
Audit all database transactions
explanation: REVOKE removes previously granted privileges from a user or role. For example: REVOKE SELECT ON employees FROM user1; removes the SELECT privilege on the employees table from user1.

q: Database audit trails are used to:
options: Speed up query execution
Record a chronological log of database activities for security review
Compress database storage
Define user access roles
explanation: Audit trails (audit logs) record a chronological sequence of database activities â€” who accessed what data, when, and what operations were performed. They are essential for security monitoring, compliance, and forensic investigation.

q: Which of the following is typically captured in a database audit trail?
options: CPU usage statistics
User login/logout events and data modification operations
Network bandwidth consumption
Disk fragmentation reports
explanation: Database audit trails typically record: user logins/logouts, DDL operations (CREATE, ALTER, DROP), DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), privilege changes, failed access attempts, and timestamps.

q: Mandatory Access Control (MAC) in database security is based on:
options: User-defined permissions on objects
Security labels/classifications assigned to data and users
Role-based group memberships
Discretionary owner-set permissions
explanation: MAC assigns security labels (classifications like TOP SECRET, SECRET, CONFIDENTIAL) to both data objects and user subjects. Access is granted only when the user's clearance level satisfies the data's classification level, enforced by the system (not data owners).

q: A cursor in SQL is used to:
options: Index a table for faster queries
Process query result sets row by row
Define foreign key relationships
Create database triggers
explanation: A cursor allows row-by-row processing of a result set returned by a SELECT query. It is a database object that points to a row in the result set and can be moved forward/backward to process each row individually.

q: Which type of cursor reflects changes made to the underlying data by other users after the cursor is opened?
options: Static cursor
Forward-only cursor
Dynamic cursor
Keyset cursor
explanation: A dynamic cursor reflects all changes (inserts, updates, deletes) made to the result set by other users while the cursor is open. This is the most expensive type in terms of resources but provides the most current view of data.

q: A static cursor in SQL:
options: Reflects all real-time changes to underlying data
Shows a snapshot of data as it was when the cursor was opened
Only moves forward through the result set
Automatically closes after each row fetch
explanation: A static cursor creates a snapshot (copy) of the result set at the time the cursor is opened. Changes made to the underlying data by other transactions after opening are NOT visible through a static cursor.

q: A forward-only cursor differs from a scrollable cursor in that:
options: It shows a snapshot of data
It can only move forward through the result set, not backward
It reflects all data changes in real time
It can only process one row at a time permanently
explanation: A forward-only cursor can only move in one direction â€” forward (FETCH NEXT). It cannot scroll backward or jump to specific rows. Scrollable cursors allow movement in any direction (FETCH PRIOR, FIRST, LAST, ABSOLUTE, RELATIVE).

q: In PL/SQL, the correct sequence for using a cursor is:
options: OPEN â†’ DECLARE â†’ FETCH â†’ CLOSE
DECLARE â†’ OPEN â†’ FETCH â†’ CLOSE
FETCH â†’ DECLARE â†’ OPEN â†’ CLOSE
DECLARE â†’ FETCH â†’ OPEN â†’ CLOSE
explanation: The correct cursor lifecycle is: DECLARE (define the cursor with a SELECT statement) â†’ OPEN (execute the query and create the result set) â†’ FETCH (retrieve rows one at a time) â†’ CLOSE (release the cursor resources).

q: A keyset cursor in SQL:
options: Creates a full snapshot of all row data at open time
Uses key values to identify rows; reflects updates and deletes but not inserts
Reflects all changes including inserts made after cursor opens
